title: Mixture of Experts — MoE Architecture, Routing & Scaling
description: Mixture of Experts (MoE) scales model capacity without proportional compute cost by activating only a subset of parameters per token. At Staff level, you must understand expert routing, load balancing, all-to-all communication, and when MoE beats dense models.

sections.title: What is MoE? — Sparse Activation at Scale
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Instead of one FFN per transformer block, MoE replaces it with multiple 'experts' — each a small FFN. A learned router (gating network) picks the top-K experts for each token. Only the selected experts run; the rest sit idle. This decouples total parameters (capacity) from active parameters (compute).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Mixtral 8x7B: 47B total params, ~13B active per token (top-2 of 8 experts). Each expert is a 5.6B FFN + shared attention layers. Quality matches Llama 2 70B on most benchmarks at 5× lower inference FLOP (13B vs 70B active). Cost ratio: 47B total is 2× more VRAM than 7B dense, but 3× more tokens/sec.

search Interview Angle: "Why not just train a 47B dense model?" A 47B dense model costs 3.6× more FLOP per token than Mixtral 8x7B (47B vs 13B active). At batch size 1 (chat), the dense model is 3.6× slower. At large batch sizes, both saturate GPU compute, but the dense model still requires 3.6× more memory bandwidth for weight reads.
sections.codeBlock:
# MoE layer replacing a standard FFN
class MoELayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff, n_experts, top_k):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_experts = n_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, n_experts, bias=False)
        # Each expert is a small SwiGLU FFN
        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([
            SwiGLUFFN(d_model, d_ff) for _ in range(n_experts)
        ])

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, D = x.shape
        # Router logits: [B, T, E]
        logits = self.router(x)
        # Top-k routing: [B, T, k] indices + weights
        weights, indices = torch.topk(logits.softmax(dim=-1), self.top_k, dim=-1)

        out = torch.zeros_like(x)
        for i, expert in enumerate(self.experts):
            # Mask: which tokens route to this expert
            mask = (indices == i).any(dim=-1)  # [B, T]
            if mask.any():
                expert_in = x[mask]
                expert_out = expert(expert_in)
                # Weighted sum: weight[i] * expert_output
                w = weights[mask][torch.where(indices[mask] == i)[1]]
                out[mask] += w.unsqueeze(-1) * expert_out
        return out

# Mixtral 8x7B: 8 experts, top-2, d_ff=14336 per expert
# Total FFN params = 8 * (3 * 4096 * 14336) ≈ 1.4B per MoE layer
# Compared to dense: 1 * (3 * 4096 * 14336) ≈ 176M
# MoE adds 7× more FFN params but only 2× more compute

sections.title: Routing & Top-K Gating — The Sparse Gate
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The router learns which experts are best for each token. Input token → linear projection → logits → softmax → top-K sparsity. Gating noise during training (Gaussian noise on logits) encourages exploration — without it, the router converges to sub-optimal expert assignments too early.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Top-2 is the empirical sweet spot. K=1 (Switch Transformer): maximum sparsity but each token gets only one expert opinion — gradient flows poorly through the single expert, and the expert collapse issue is worse. K=4+: quality approaches dense but loses the MoE compute advantage. With E=64 experts, top-2 means 97% of parameters are idle — that's the efficiency win.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Start with noisy top-k gating: logits += N(0, 1/noise_scale) during training. Noise scale = 1.0 (Shazeer default). Anneal noise to 0 at end of training. Use K=2 for most setups. K=1 for extreme throughput needs. Never use K > 4.
sections.codeBlock:
# Noisy top-k gating (Shazeer et al. 2017)
import torch

def noisy_top_k_gating(x, router_weight, n_experts, k=2, noise_scale=1.0, training=True):
    """
    x: [batch, dim] — token representations
    Returns: [batch, n_experts] weights (sparse)
    """
    logits = x @ router_weight  # [batch, n_experts]

    if training and noise_scale > 0:
        # Gaussian noise encourages exploration
        noise = torch.randn_like(logits) * noise_scale
        logits = logits + noise

    # Softmax over all experts
    weights = logits.softmax(dim=-1)

    # Top-k masking: keep only top-k, zero the rest
    top_k_vals, top_k_idx = torch.topk(weights, k, dim=-1)
    mask = torch.zeros_like(weights).scatter_(-1, top_k_idx, 1.0)
    weights = weights * mask

    # Renormalize to sum to 1 (over the k selected experts)
    weights = weights / weights.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    return weights, top_k_idx

# ponytail: real impl uses einsum for efficiency, not explicit loop
# Ceiling: noise anneal schedule needs tuning per architecture

sections.title: Load Balancing & Auxiliary Loss — Preventing Expert Collapse
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Without intervention, the router converges to sending nearly all tokens to 1-2 experts. The other experts atrophy (never trained) — this is expert collapse. The fix: an auxiliary loss that penalizes uneven expert utilization.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Auxiliary loss coefficient = 0.01 (standard across MoE papers). The loss = n_experts × Σ(f_i × P_i) where f_i = fraction of tokens routed to expert i, P_i = average router probability for expert i. Uniform assignment gives loss = 1.0. Collapse (all tokens to one expert) gives loss = n_experts. Without this loss, MoE training fails within 100 steps — router logits diverge and training loss explodes.

search Interview Angle: "The auxiliary loss conflicts with the primary training loss — how do you balance?" The auxiliary loss is intentionally small (0.01 coefficient). It acts as a regularizer, not a competing objective. If you see expert collapse, increase coefficient to 0.02. If you see quality degradation from over-regularization, decrease to 0.005. Z-loss (penalizing large router logits) is complementary — it keeps the router in a stable numerical range.
sections.codeBlock:
# Load balancing auxiliary loss (Switch Transformer style)
def load_balancing_loss(router_logits, expert_indices, n_experts):
    """
    router_logits: [batch, n_experts] — pre-softmax
    expert_indices: [batch] — chosen expert per token
    """
    B = router_logits.shape[0]

    # f_i: fraction of tokens routed to each expert
    f_i = torch.zeros(n_experts, device=router_logits.device)
    f_i.scatter_add_(0, expert_indices,
                     torch.ones(B, device=router_logits.device))
    f_i = f_i / B

    # P_i: avg router probability assigned to each expert
    router_probs = router_logits.softmax(dim=-1)
    P_i = router_probs.mean(dim=0)

    # Loss = n_experts * sum(f_i * P_i)
    # Uniform: f_i = 1/E, P_i = 1/E → loss = E * E * (1/E²) = 1.0
    # Collapse: f_0 = 1, P_0 = 1 → loss = E * 1 * 1 = E
    loss = n_experts * (f_i * P_i).sum()
    return loss

# Z-loss: penalizes large router logits for stability
# (used in Switch Transformer + GShard)
def z_loss(router_logits, coefficient=1e-4):
    """
    Encourage router logits to stay small (centered near 0).
    Without this, router logits can grow to 100+ leading to NaN.
    """
    return coefficient * router_logits.square().mean()

# Total loss = nll + 0.01 * lb_loss + 1e-4 * z_loss

sections.title: All-to-All Communication — The MoE Bottleneck
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: In distributed MoE, each GPU holds a subset of experts. When a token on GPU 0 routes to an expert on GPU 3, the token must be sent (with its hidden state) to GPU 3 via all-to-all communication. After the expert processes it, the result comes back. The communication cost grows linearly with the number of experts.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Mixtral 8x7B on 8 GPUs with 1 expert per GPU: all-to-all overhead is ~5-10% of step time. Each token's hidden state (4096 floats) must be sent to 1-2 GPUs and received back. At 64 experts across 64 GPUs: overhead can be 30-50% — each GPU sends tokens to 2 of 64 destinations (random access pattern). Solution: overlap communication with expert computation using double-buffered all-to-all.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Profile your all-to-all vs compute ratio. If communication dominates: 1) Increase tokens per GPU (larger batch size hides latency). 2) Use expert parallelism + tensor parallelism hybrid (GShard style). 3) Overlap: compute experts on local tokens while all-to-all for remote tokens is in-flight. 4) For >64 experts, consider expert sharding (each expert split across multiple GPUs).
sections.codeBlock:
# All-to-all communication in distributed MoE (conceptual)
def moe_all_to_all_send_recv(hidden_states, expert_assignment, world_size, rank):
    """
    hidden_states: [local_tokens, d_model] — this GPU's tokens
    expert_assignment: [local_tokens] — expert IDs per token

    All-to-all: each rank sends tokens to the rank that has their expert.
    """
    # Group tokens by destination rank
    send_bufs = [[] for _ in range(world_size)]
    for i, expert_id in enumerate(expert_assignment):
        dst_rank = expert_id % world_size  # expert-to-rank mapping
        send_bufs[dst_rank].append(hidden_states[i])

    # Concatenate per destination
    send_bufs = [torch.stack(buf) if buf else torch.empty(0, hidden_states.size(-1))
                 for buf in send_bufs]

    # All-to-all communication
    recv_bufs = [torch.empty_like(buf) for buf in send_bufs]
    # torch.distributed.all_to_all(recv_bufs, send_bufs)
    # ponytail: real impl uses NCCL all-to-all with CUDA streams

    return recv_bufs

# Communication overhead grows linearly with n_experts.
# For Mixtral 8x7B on 8 GPUs: each GPU sends ~12.5% of tokens
# to each of 2 remote targets (top-2 routing).
# ponytail: for 64+ experts, use hierarchical all-to-all (torus topology)

sections.title: Expert Capacity & Token Dropping — Graceful Degradation
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Each expert has a fixed slot count (capacity). If too many tokens route to the same expert, the excess tokens are dropped — their hidden state passes through unchanged (like a residual skip). Capacity prevents any single expert from becoming a bottleneck and ensures predictable compute per batch.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Capacity = tokens_per_batch × capacity_factor / n_experts. Standard capacity_factor = 1.0-1.25. At 1.0, each expert gets exactly its proportional share. At 1.25, 25% overflow headroom. Drop rate target < 1%. If drop rate > 5%, increase capacity_factor. Mixtral 8x7B uses capacity_factor = 1.0 with < 0.5% drop rate in practice.

search Interview Angle: "Doesn't dropping tokens hurt quality?" Yes, but < 1% drops are negligible. If dropping occurs, the token's representation is weaker (missing expert refinement) but the residual connection preserves the layer input. GShard showed that up to 5% drops don't significantly hurt downstream tasks. The alternative — no capacity limit — allows one expert to hog compute, creating unpredictable latency. Capacity + drop is the lesser evil.
sections.codeBlock:
# Expert capacity enforcement with token dropping
class CapacityMoE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff, n_experts, top_k, capacity_factor=1.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_experts = n_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        self.capacity_factor = capacity_factor
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, n_experts, bias=False)
        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([
            SwiGLUFFN(d_model, d_ff) for _ in range(n_experts)
        ])

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, D = x.shape
        N = B * T  # total tokens
        capacity = int(N * self.capacity_factor / self.n_experts)

        # Get top-k routing decisions
        weights, indices = torch.topk(
            self.router(x).softmax(dim=-1), self.top_k, dim=-1)

        out = torch.zeros_like(x)
        for i, expert in enumerate(self.experts):
            # Which tokens (across top-k) chose this expert?
            positions = (indices == i).nonzero(as_tuple=True)
            tokens, k_idx = positions[0], positions[1]

            if len(tokens) == 0:
                continue

            # Capacity limit: only process first `capacity` tokens
            if len(tokens) > capacity:
                tokens = tokens[:capacity]
                k_idx = k_idx[:capacity]
                # Remaining tokens are dropped (handled implicitly)
                # ponytail: log drop rate for monitoring

            # Process selected tokens (only up to capacity)
            expert_in = x[tokens.flatten()].view(-1, D)
            expert_out = expert(expert_in)

            # Apply routing weight
            w = weights[tokens.flatten()].view(-1)
            out[tokens.flatten()] += w.unsqueeze(-1) * expert_out

        # Dropped tokens: their `out` stays as 0 (identity through residual)
        return out

# Monitor: if drop_rate > 0.05, increase capacity_factor
# Re-route strategy: re-assign dropped tokens to next-best expert

sections.title: MoE vs Dense — Decision Framework for Production
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: MoE and dense models occupy different points on the efficiency-quality Pareto frontier. The choice depends on serving workload, latency budget, and hardware. This section provides a concrete framework to make that call.

bar_chart When MoE wins: 1) Moderate-to-large batch serving (chat, API with batching) — MoE's active-param advantage shines. 2) Memory-constrained (mobile, edge, single GPU) — 47B total but 13B active = 70B quality on 1 GPU. 3) Multi-task models — different experts can specialize for different domains (code, math, creative). 4) Training cost dominated by data parallelism — MoE's expert parallelism adds less overhead than model parallelism for dense 70B.

When dense wins: 1) Very small batch (latency-critical < 50ms) — all-to-all overhead dominates. 2) Single-task, data-rich (language understanding on fixed domain) — one dense FFN is enough. 3) Hardware without fast interconnects — MoE needs NVLink or InfiniBand; on Ethernet, all-to-all kills performance.

track_changes Hybrid approach (DeepSeek V2): First 3 layers dense (learn universal features), remaining 55 layers MoE (specialized refinement). This gives 70B-quality at ~20B active params with better training stability than pure MoE.

search Staff Engineer Question: "500M queries/day chatbot. Dense 70B costs $100K/mo on 16 A100s. Same budget — should you switch to MoE?" Yes. MoE 8x7B: 47B total fits on 4 A100s (INT4), serves at 2× throughput = 8 instances on 4 A100s. Your 16 GPUs serve 4× the traffic at same cost. Quality is within 1-2% on chat benchmarks. MoE wins.
sections.codeBlock:
# MoE vs Dense: active parameter comparison
import math

def moe_vs_dense(total_params_b, active_params_b, dense_params_b, batch_size):
    """
    Compare FLOP per token for MoE vs dense at varying batch sizes.
    """
    # Active params determine compute
    moe_flop = active_params_b * 2  # 1 FLOP per param per token (rough)
    dense_flop = dense_params_b * 2

    # All-to-all overhead: proportional to total_params (communication)
    # Scales with batch: more tokens = more data to transfer
    aa_overhead = 0.05 * batch_size * total_params_b  # 5% of total per batch

    print(f"MoE   active: {active_params_b:.0f}B, total: {total_params_b:.0f}B")
    print(f"Dense active: {dense_params_b:.0f}B, total: {dense_params_b:.0f}B")
    print(f"Batch={batch_size}: MoE {moe_flop:.0f}G FLOP/token, "
          f"Dense {dense_flop:.0f}G FLOP/token")
    print(f"All-to-all overhead: {aa_overhead:.1f}G per batch")
    print(f"MoE wins: {'Yes' if moe_flop + aa_overhead/batch_size < dense_flop else 'No'}")

# Compare at small vs large batch
print("=== Small batch (1) ===")
moe_vs_dense(47, 13, 70, 1)
print("\n=== Large batch (1024) ===")
moe_vs_dense(47, 13, 70, 1024)
# At batch=1, MoE compute is 13/70 = 18% of dense
# At batch=1024, all-to-all overhead erodes some advantage
# ponytail: exact break-even depends on interconnect bandwidth

sections.title: Dense Models — When and Why to Go Dense
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Dense models use a single FFN per transformer block where every parameter is active for every token. There's no router, no expert selection, no all-to-all communication. What you see (parameter count) is what you pay (compute per token). This simplicity is the dense model's superpower — and its limitation.

bar_chart When dense wins over MoE: 1) Latency-critical serving < 50ms: At batch size 1, all parameters are resident on the GPU and there's zero communication overhead. MoE must pay all-to-all latency even for a single token. 2) Single-task domains: If your model only needs to excel at one thing (code completion or sentiment analysis), a dense model's full capacity per token beats MoE's 13B/47B active split. 3) Hardware without fast interconnects: MoE on Ethernet (< 100GbE) spends 30-50% step time in all-to-all. Dense models only need model parallelism which is more bandwidth-efficient.

bar_chart Notable dense model families: Llama 3 (8B, 70B, 405B — all dense), GPT-4 (1.8T alleged, 8 experts × 220B — unconfirmed; officially treated as dense for API purposes), BERT (encoder-only dense, 110M-340M), T5 (encoder-decoder dense, 220M-11B). Every frontier lab has both dense and MoE models — they're complementary tools.

bar_chart Concrete numbers — memory & hardware comparison: Llama 3 405B dense: 810GB in FP16 (405B × 2 bytes). Requires 8 × H100 (80GB each) with tensor parallelism. Mixtral 8x7B MoE: 47B total params, 13B active, fits 2 × H100 at FP16 or 1 × H100 at INT4. The tradeoff: MoE gives more knowledge (total params) at less compute (active params), while dense gives all its parameters to every reasoning step. For math, logic, and multi-step reasoning benchmarks, dense models often outperform MoE at the same active-param budget.

psychology The 'knowledge vs reasoning' tradeoff: MoE's total parameter count represents stored knowledge (facts, patterns, linguistic rules in different experts). Its active parameter count represents reasoning depth. Dense models, with 100% of params active per token, dedicate more FLOP to reasoning at each step. On MMLU (knowledge), MoE 8x7B ≈ Llama 2 70B. On MATH (reasoning), dense models with the same active param count consistently lead.

search Staff Engineer insight: "Dense is preferred when you control the full stack and latency is the #1 metric." At FAANG, the choice is driven by serving economics: if your serving fleet is homogeneous (all H100, all NVLink, predictable batch sizes), MoE's complexity often isn't worth it. Dense models are simpler to deploy, easier to debug (no expert collapse, no load balancing), and have more predictable performance. MoE is the choice when you need 70B-quality on a single GPU or when training compute budget is the bottleneck.

search Interview Angle: "Your startup has 4 H100s and needs a coding assistant with <100ms latency. Dense or MoE?" Use dense — 4 H100s can serve Llama 3 70B with tensor parallelism (4-way TP), achieving <100ms per token. A MoE 8x7B would need all-to-all across 4 GPUs, adding 15-25ms communication overhead per token, pushing you past the latency budget. MoE's all-to-all advantage only kicks in at batch sizes > 4 or when you need 405B-quality on limited GPUs.
sections.codeBlock:
# Dense FFN vs MoE forward pass comparison
import torch, torch.nn as nn

class DenseFFN(nn.Module):
    """Dense FFN: all params active for every token."""
    def __init__(self, d_model=4096, d_ff=14336):
        super().__init__()
        self.gate = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
        self.up   = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
        self.down = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        # 100% params compute for every token
        return self.down(F.silu(self.gate(x)) * self.up(x))
    # FLOP per token: 2 * d_model * d_ff * 3 (gate+up+down)
    # = 2 * 4096 * 14336 * 3 ≈ 352M FLOP

class MoEFFN(nn.Module):
    """MoE: only top-2 of 8 experts compute per token."""
    def __init__(self, d_model=4096, d_ff=14336, n_experts=8, top_k=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, n_experts, bias=False)
        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([
            DenseFFN(d_model, d_ff) for _ in range(n_experts)
        ])
        self.top_k = top_k

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, D = x.shape
        logits = self.router(x)  # [B, T, E]
        weights, indices = torch.topk(logits.softmax(dim=-1), self.top_k, dim=-1)

        out = torch.zeros_like(x)
        for i, expert in enumerate(self.experts):
            mask = (indices == i).any(dim=-1)  # [B, T]
            if mask.any():
                w = weights[mask][torch.where(indices[mask] == i)[1]]
                out[mask] += w.unsqueeze(-1) * expert(x[mask])
        return out
    # FLOP per token: 2 * d_model * d_ff * top_k (13B active)
    # = 2 * 4096 * 14336 * 2 ≈ 235M FLOP (33% less than dense!)

# Key insight: MoE uses 33% less FLOP per token at same d_ff
# because only top-k experts compute. But total params are 8× more.
# ponytail: this comparison ignores attention FLOP which are identical

sections.title: References & Key Papers
sections.description:
• Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer (Shazeer et al., 2017) — The original MoE paper: noisy top-k gating, auxiliary loss, 137B model with 1000 experts
  • GShard: Scaling Giant Models with Conditional Computation and Automatic Sharding (Lepikhin et al., 2020) — Google's distributed MoE for 600B translation model, expert parallelism, top-2 gating at scale
  • Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models with Simple and Efficient Sparsity (Fedus et al., 2022) — Switch to top-1 gating for maximum sparsity, 1.6T model, load balancing loss
  • Mixtral of Experts (Mistral AI, 2024) — 8x7B MoE matching Llama 2 70B quality, practical deployment guide
  • DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model (DeepSeek, 2024) — Hybrid dense+MoE architecture, Multi-Head Latent Attention, 236B total with 21B active
  • Scaling Vision Transformers with MoE — MoE applied to ViT, scaling laws for sparse models

details:
MoE Decision Flow — When to Use Sparse Models:

  1. Latency budget > 100ms? → Yes: MoE viable. No (< 50ms): prefer dense to avoid all-to-all overhead at small batch.
  2. Single GPU deployment? → MoE enables 70B-quality on 1 GPU (INT4 MoE ≈ 24GB). Dense 70B needs 2-4 GPUs.
  3. Multi-task (chat, code, math, retrieval)? → MoE experts can specialize. Dense models are less flexible per-param.
  4. Batch size > 4? → MoE's all-to-all amortizes. For batch 1 streaming, the overhead per-token is highest.
  5. Interconnect bandwidth? → NVLink 600GB/s+ → MoE is fine. Ethernet < 100GbE → dense may be faster.
  6. Cost-optimal at scale: MoE 8x7B serves 3× more queries than dense 70B at same GPU budget (active params 13B vs 70B, 5.4× advantage partially offset by all-to-all + total VRAM).

Bottom line: MoE is the default choice for deployed LLM serving at scale (millions of queries/day). Dense is for latency-critical edge cases and research simplicity. The industry trajectory points toward hybrid architectures (DeepSeek V2, Qwen2-MoE) that use dense early layers for universal features and MoE later layers for specialized refinement.
